title.zh: 卷八十六 何武王嘉師丹傳
title.en: Volume 86: He Wu, Wang Jia and Shi Dan
title.raw: 卷八十六 何武王嘉師丹傳 Volume 86: He Wu, Wang Jia and Shi Dan

sentences.zh: 卷八十六 何武王嘉師丹傳第五十六

translations.literal: Scroll 86: Biography of He Wu, Wang Jia, and Shi Dan, number fifty-six.
translations.idiomatic: Book 86—the fifty-sixth memoir: He Wu, Wang Jia, and Shi Dan.

sentences.zh: 何武字君公，蜀郡郫縣人也。

translations.literal: He Wu, styled Jungong, was a man of Pi County in Shu Commandery.
translations.idiomatic: He Wu, courtesy name Jungong, was a native of Pi County in the Shu commandery.

sentences.zh: 宣帝時，天下和平，四夷賓服，神爵、五鳳之間婁蒙瑞應。

translations.literal: In the time of Emperor Xuan, all under Heaven was peaceful; the four Yi submitted as guests; between the Shenjue and Wufeng reign periods repeatedly they received auspicious responses.
translations.idiomatic: Under Emperor Xuan the realm was at peace, the peoples beyond the borders came as tributaries, and throughout the Shenjue and Wufeng eras Heaven time and again answered with propitious omens.

sentences.zh: 而益州刺史王襄使辯士王褒頌漢德，作中和、樂職、宣布詩三篇。

translations.literal: The Regional Inspector of Yi, Wang Xiang, caused the rhetorician Wang Bao to praise Han virtue and compose three poems: Zhonghe, Lezhi, and Xuanbu.
translations.idiomatic: The inspector of Yi, Wang Xiang, sent the scholar Wang Bao to celebrate the virtue of the Han court; Wang produced the three odes "Harmony," "Joy in Office," and "The Proclamation."

sentences.zh: 武年十四五，與成都楊覆眾等共習歌之。

translations.literal: When Wu was fourteen or fifteen, together with Yang Fuzhong of Chengdu and others they jointly practiced singing them.
translations.idiomatic: At fourteen or fifteen, Wu joined Yang Fuzhong of Chengdu and their circle in learning those songs by heart.

sentences.zh: 是時，宣帝循武帝故事，求通達茂異士，召見武等於宣室。

translations.literal: At this time Emperor Xuan followed Emperor Wu's precedent, sought penetrating and outstandingly exceptional scholars, and summoned Wu and others for audience in the Xuanshi Hall.
translations.idiomatic: Then Emperor Xuan, following the example of Emperor Wu, called for men of insight and rare talent and received Wu and his fellows in the Xuanshi Hall.

sentences.zh: 上曰：「此盛德之事，吾何足以當之哉！」

translations.literal: The sovereign said: "This is an affair of abundant virtue; how am I sufficient to deserve it!"
translations.idiomatic: The emperor said, "Such deeds belong to the highest virtue; how could I possibly be worthy of them?"

sentences.zh: 以褒為待詔，武等賜帛罷。

translations.literal: He made Bao an Expectant Official; Wu and the others were granted silk and dismissed.
translations.idiomatic: Wang Bao was appointed expectant at court; Wu and the rest were given silk and sent home.

sentences.zh: 武詣博士受業，治易。

translations.literal: Wu went to an Erudite to receive instruction and studied the Changes.
translations.idiomatic: He enrolled under an academician and took up the study of the Book of Changes.

sentences.zh: 以射策甲科為郎，與翟方進交志相友。

translations.literal: By archery-examination in the first class he became a Gentleman and with Zhai Fangjin exchanged aims and were friends.
translations.idiomatic: He passed the highest tier of the written examination and was made a court gentleman; he and Zhai Fangjin became close friends who shared the same ambitions.

sentences.zh: 光祿勳舉四行，遷為鄠令，坐法免歸。

translations.literal: The Superintendent of the Imperial Household recommended him for the Four Conducts; he was transferred to be Magistrate of Hu, and for an offense against the law was dismissed and returned home.
translations.idiomatic: The superintendent of the household nominated him for the "four virtues" examination; he was promoted to magistrate of Hu, then removed from office for a legal infraction and went home.

sentences.zh: 武兄弟五人，皆為郡吏，郡縣敬憚之。

translations.literal: Wu's brothers were five persons; all were commandery clerks; the commandery and counties revered and feared them.
translations.idiomatic: Wu had four brothers; all five brothers held posts in the commandery administration, and officials throughout the county and commandery stood in awe of them.

sentences.zh: 武弟顯家有巿籍，租常不入，縣數負其課。

translations.literal: Wu's younger brother Xian's household had market registration; rent constantly did not enter; the county repeatedly was in arrears on his levies.
translations.idiomatic: His brother Xian's family was registered as market traders, so their rent payments were habitually late and the county repeatedly fell short on the taxes owed from them.

sentences.zh: 巿嗇夫求商捕辱顯家，顯怒，欲以吏事中商。

translations.literal: The market overseer Qiu Shang arrested and humiliated Xian's household; Xian was angry and wished by clerk means to strike at Shang.
translations.idiomatic: The market clerk Qiu Shang seized Xian's people and publicly shamed them; Xian, furious, plotted to use his official connections to ruin Shang.

sentences.zh: 武曰：「以吾家租賦繇役不為眾先，奉公吏不亦宜乎！」

translations.literal: Wu said: "In that our household's rent, taxes, and corvée labor are not ahead of the multitude, for a clerk who serves the public is it not also fitting!"
translations.idiomatic: Wu said, "Our family has never tried to skip ahead of others on rent, taxes, or labor service. A man who serves the public in office should accept the same—is that not only right?"

sentences.zh: 武卒白太守，召商為卒吏，州里聞之皆服焉。

translations.literal: Wu finally reported to the Grand Administrator, who summoned Shang to be a clerk-soldier; when the district heard it, all submitted.
translations.idiomatic: Wu went to the grand administrator, who then appointed Shang as a clerk in the commandery office; the whole locality heard of it and conceded that Wu had acted rightly.

sentences.zh: 久之，太僕王音舉武賢良方正，徵對策，拜為諫大夫，遷揚州刺史。

translations.literal: After a long time, the Grand Coachman Wang Yin recommended Wu as Worthy and Upright; he was summoned for policy response, was appointed Grandee Remonstrant, and was transferred to be Regional Inspector of Yang.
translations.idiomatic: Eventually the grand coachman Wang Yin nominated him as "worthy and upright"; he was called to the capital to answer policy questions, appointed grandee remonstrant, and then made regional inspector of Yang province.

sentences.zh: 所舉奏二千石長吏必先露章，服罪者為虧除，免之而已；

translations.literal: In what he memorialized impeaching two-thousand-dan senior officials, he always first exposed the document; those who acknowledged guilt he treated as having a deficit removed and dismissed them only;
translations.idiomatic: Whenever he impeached a senior official of two-thousand-dan rank, he first made the charges public; if the man admitted fault, Wu would strike only the minimum demerit and limit the penalty to dismissal.

sentences.zh: 不服，極法奏之，抵罪或至死。

translations.literal: if they did not submit, he memorialized them to the utmost of the law, and they met punishment or even unto death.
translations.idiomatic: If the official refused to admit guilt, Wu would press the memorial to the limit of the statutes, so that the man might face conviction or even death.

sentences.zh: 九江太守戴聖，禮經號小戴者也，行治多不法，前刺史以其大儒，優容之。

translations.literal: Dai Sheng, Grand Administrator of Jiujiang, was he who in the Rites Classics was called Lesser Dai; in conduct of government there was much that was not lawful; the former Regional Inspector, because he was a great Ru, treated him with indulgent tolerance.
translations.idiomatic: Dai Sheng of Jiujiang—the "Lesser Dai" of the ritual canon—ran his administration with many irregularities; the previous inspector had looked the other way because Dai was a celebrated scholar.

sentences.zh: 及武為刺史，行部錄囚徒，有所舉以屬郡。

translations.literal: When Wu became Regional Inspector, on circuit he recorded prisoners; there was something he recommended and entrusted to the commandery.
translations.idiomatic: When Wu took office as inspector, he toured the province, reviewed prisoners, and referred certain findings to the commandery for action.

sentences.zh: 聖曰：「後進生何知，乃欲亂人治！」

translations.literal: Sheng said: "What does a junior student know, that he wishes to disorder others' governance!"
translations.idiomatic: Dai Sheng snapped, "What can a junior upstart know that he dares meddle with another man's government?"

sentences.zh: 皆無所決。

translations.literal: In all cases there was nothing decided.
translations.idiomatic: Nothing was ever resolved.

sentences.zh: 武使從事廉得其罪，聖懼，自免。

translations.literal: Wu caused an Attendant Clerk to investigate and obtain his crimes; Sheng feared and dismissed himself.
translations.idiomatic: Wu sent an aide to gather proof of Dai's wrongdoing; Dai grew afraid and resigned his post.

sentences.zh: 後為博士，毀武於朝廷。

translations.literal: Afterward he became an Erudite and slandered Wu at the court.
translations.idiomatic: Dai Sheng later became an academician and maligned Wu before the whole court.

sentences.zh: 武聞之，終不揚其惡。

translations.literal: When Wu heard it, to the end he did not broadcast his evil.
translations.idiomatic: Wu heard of it but never retaliated by spreading word of Dai's misconduct.

sentences.zh: 而聖子賓客為群盜，得，繫廬江，聖自以子必死。

translations.literal: But Sheng's son's retainers acted as a band of robbers; they were captured and bound in Lujiang; Sheng himself thought his son would certainly die.
translations.idiomatic: Then retainers of Dai Sheng's son turned to banditry; they were caught and jailed in Lujiang, and Dai assumed his son was as good as dead.

sentences.zh: 武平心決之，卒得不死。

translations.literal: Wu with level mind decided it; in the end he obtained not death.
translations.idiomatic: Wu judged the case with impartial care and spared the young man's life.

sentences.zh: 自是後，聖慚服。

translations.literal: From this afterward, Sheng was ashamed and submitted.
translations.idiomatic: After that Dai Sheng was humbled and conceded Wu's superiority.

sentences.zh: 武每奏事至京師，聖未嘗不造門謝恩。

translations.literal: Whenever Wu on memorial business came to the capital, Sheng never failed to go to his gate to thank for grace.
translations.idiomatic: Whenever Wu came to the capital on official business, Dai Sheng would call at his door without fail to express his gratitude.

sentences.zh: 武為刺史，二千石有罪，應時舉奏，其餘賢與不肖敬之如一，是以郡國各重其守相，州中清平。

translations.literal: When Wu was Regional Inspector, if two-thousand-dan had guilt, he timely memorialized impeachment; as for the rest, worthy or unworthy, he revered them as one; therefore commanderies and kingdoms each valued their governors and chancellors, and within the province it was pure and peaceful.
translations.idiomatic: As inspector, Wu promptly memorialized any two-thousand-dan official who broke the law, yet toward everyone else—capable or not—he showed the same respect; commanderies therefore took their governors seriously, and the province stayed orderly and calm.

sentences.zh: 行部必先即學官見諸生，試其誦論，問以得失，然後入傳舍，出記問墾田頃畝，五穀美惡，已乃見二千石，以為常。

translations.literal: On circuit he always first went to the school officials and met the students, tested their recitations and disquisitions, and questioned them on gain and loss; afterward he entered the relay inn, issued registers and asked about reclaimed fields in qing and mu, the good and bad of the five grains; only then did he see the two-thousand-dan; he took this as constant practice.
translations.idiomatic: On his rounds he always began at the local academy: he met the students, heard their lessons and debates, and asked what was working and what was not. Only then did he retire to the post-house, send out written inquiries on acreage under cultivation and the condition of the crops, and finally summon the two-thousand-dan official. This became his fixed routine.

sentences.zh: 初，武為郡吏時，事太守何壽。

translations.literal: At the beginning, when Wu was a commandery clerk, he served Grand Administrator He Shou.
translations.idiomatic: In his early years, while still a commandery clerk, Wu served under Grand Administrator He Shou.

sentences.zh: 壽知武有宰相器，以其同姓故厚之。

translations.literal: Shou knew Wu had the vessel of a chancellor; because they shared the same surname, therefore he treated him generously.
translations.idiomatic: Shou saw in Wu the makings of a chief minister and, sharing the surname He, favored him accordingly.

sentences.zh: 後壽為大司農，其兄子為廬江長史。

translations.literal: Afterward Shou became Grand Minister of Agriculture; his elder brother's son was Chief Clerk of Lujiang.
translations.idiomatic: Shou was later promoted to grand minister of agriculture; his nephew held the post of chief clerk in Lujiang.

sentences.zh: 時武奏事在邸，壽兄子適在長安，壽為具召武弟顯及故人楊覆眾等，酒酣，見其兄子，曰：「此子揚州長史，材能駑下，未嘗省見。」

translations.literal: At the time Wu on memorial business was at the lodging; Shou's elder brother's son happened to be in Chang'an; Shou prepared a feast and summoned Wu's younger brother Xian and old acquaintance Yang Fuzhong and others; when the wine was merry, he saw his elder brother's son and said: "This boy is Yangzhou Chief Clerk; his talent and ability are inferior; I have never examined and met him."
translations.idiomatic: Once, while Wu was staying at the capital lodge on official business, Shou's nephew happened to be in Chang'an. Shou gave a banquet for Wu's brother Xian, Yang Fuzhong, and other friends, and when the wine was flowing he gestured toward his nephew and said, "There is my boy—the chief clerk of Yang province. Mediocre talent, and I have hardly bothered to see him."

sentences.zh: 顯等甚慚，退以謂武，武曰：「刺史古之方伯，上所委任，一州表率也，職在進善退惡。

translations.literal: Xian and the others were very ashamed; retiring they spoke to Wu; Wu said: "The Regional Inspector is in antiquity the Fangbo; what the superiors entrust, the model of a whole province; the duty lies in advancing the good and retiring the bad.
translations.idiomatic: Xian and the others were mortified; afterward they told Wu. Wu replied, "A regional inspector is the old regional lord: the throne's delegate and the moral example for an entire province. His job is to promote the worthy and remove the corrupt.

sentences.zh: 吏治行有茂異，民有隱逸，乃當召見，不可有所私問。」

translations.literal: When clerks' governance conduct has luxuriant excellence, or among the people there are recluses, then one ought summon audience; one may not have private questioning."
translations.idiomatic: Only when an official's record shows outstanding merit, or when a recluse of real note appears among the people, should he be summoned for interview—not for private favoritism."

sentences.zh: 顯、覆眾強之，不得已召見，賜卮酒。

translations.literal: Xian and Fuzhong forced Wu; having no choice Wu summoned audience and bestowed a cup of wine.
translations.idiomatic: Xian and Yang Fuzhong pressed Wu until he gave in, received the nephew in audience, and presented him with a cup of wine.

sentences.zh: 歲中，廬江太守舉之。

translations.literal: Within the year, the Grand Administrator of Lujiang recommended him.
translations.idiomatic: Before the year was out the grand administrator of Lujiang had nominated the nephew for promotion.

sentences.zh: 其守法見憚如此。

translations.literal: In his guarding of the law he was feared thus.
translations.idiomatic: Such was the rigor with which he upheld the law that even friends learned to fear crossing him.

sentences.zh: 武為人仁厚，好進士，獎稱人之善。

translations.literal: In person Wu was humane and generous; he loved to advance scholars and praised and called people's good points.
translations.idiomatic: Wu was warm and generous by nature; he delighted in promoting able men and was quick to praise others' virtues.

sentences.zh: 為楚內史厚兩龔，在沛郡厚兩唐，及為公卿，薦之朝廷。

translations.literal: As Inner Scribe of Chu he treated generously the two Gongs; in Pei Commandery he treated generously the two Tangs; when he became a duke or minister, he recommended them to the court.
translations.idiomatic: As inner scribe of Chu he befriended the two Gong brothers; as administrator of Pei he did the same for the two Tang brothers; once he reached the highest offices he brought all four forward at court.

sentences.zh: 此人顯於世者，何侯力也，世以此多焉。

translations.literal: That these men became illustrious in the generation was the Marquis of He's force; the generation for this praised him.
translations.idiomatic: Their later fame was owed to Marquis He; for that the world thought the more of him.

sentences.zh: 然疾朋黨，問文吏必於儒者，問儒者必於文吏，以相參檢。

translations.literal: Yet he hated factional cliques; when questioning document clerks he always did so at Ru scholars; when questioning Ru scholars he always did so at document clerks, thereby mutually cross-examining.
translations.idiomatic: Yet he detested cliques: he would cross-check every legal specialist with a classicist and every classicist with a legal man so that neither side could hide the truth.

sentences.zh: 欲除吏，先為科例以防請託。

translations.literal: When wishing to appoint clerks, he first made regulations and precedents to guard against requests and entreaties.
translations.idiomatic: Before any appointment he published clear rules and precedents to block favor-seeking.

sentences.zh: 其所居亦無赫赫名，去後常見思。

translations.literal: In what places he dwelt there was also no blazing fame; after he departed he was constantly thought of.
translations.idiomatic: While in office he never sought a flashy reputation, yet after he left, people often missed him.

sentences.zh: 及為御史大夫司空，與丞相方進共奏言：「往者諸侯王斷獄治政，內史典獄事，相總綱紀輔王，中尉備盜賊。

translations.literal: When he became Imperial Counselor and Minister of Works, together with Chancellor Fangjin they jointly memorialized saying: "In former times the feudal kings judged cases and governed policy; the Inner Scribe managed prison affairs; the Chancellor summed the guiding threads and assisted the king; the Commandant of the Capital stood ready against robbers and bandits.
translations.idiomatic: After Wu rose to imperial counselor and minister of works, he and Chancellor Zhai Fangjin jointly submitted: "Formerly kings of principalities heard lawsuits and ran their domains: the inner scribe handled penal matters, the chancellor held the reins and advised the king, and the commandant of the capital guarded against crime.

sentences.zh: 今王不斷獄與政，中尉官罷，職并內史，郡國守相委任，所以壹統信，安百姓也。

translations.literal: Now the king does not judge cases and participate in government; the Commandant of the Capital office is abolished; duties merge into the Inner Scribe; commandery and kingdom governors and chancellors are entrusted—this is the means to unify trust and pacify the hundred surnames.
translations.idiomatic: Today the kings no longer hear cases or share in government; the commandancy has been abolished and its duties folded into the inner scribe, while commandery governors hold delegated authority—all intended to unify confidence in the law and give the people security.

sentences.zh: 今內史位卑而權重，威職相踰，不統尊者，難以為治。

translations.literal: Now the Inner Scribe's position is low yet power is heavy; awe and duty mutually exceed; not being unified in what is honored, it is difficult thereby to govern.
translations.idiomatic: Yet the inner scribe now stands low in rank while wielding heavy power; authority and office no longer match, and nothing senior unifies the structure—governance suffers for it.

sentences.zh: 臣請相如太守，內史如都尉，以順尊卑之序，平輕重之權。」

translations.literal: Your servants request that the Chancellor be like the Grand Administrator, the Inner Scribe like the Commandant, thereby to accord with the order of honored and humble and level the weight of light and heavy authority."
translations.idiomatic: We therefore ask that the chancellor be ranked like a grand administrator and the inner scribe like a commandant, so that precedence is clear and power is balanced."

sentences.zh: 制曰：「可。」

translations.literal: The edict said: "Permitted."
translations.idiomatic: The edict read: "Granted."

sentences.zh: 以內史為中尉。

translations.literal: They made the Inner Scribe be the Commandant of the Capital.
translations.idiomatic: The inner scribe was redesignated commandant of the capital.

sentences.zh: 初武為九卿時，奏言宜置三公官，又與方進共奏罷刺史，更置州牧，後皆復復故，語在朱博傳。

translations.literal: At the beginning when Wu was among the Nine Ministers, he memorialized saying it was fitting to establish the Three Dukes offices; also together with Fangjin he jointly memorialized to abolish Regional Inspectors and replace them with Pastoralists; afterward all repeatedly reverted to the former; the words are in Zhu Bo's biography.
translations.idiomatic: Earlier, while Wu still held one of the nine minister posts, he had urged creation of the three excellencies and, with Fangjin, had memorialized to abolish regional inspectors in favor of provincial shepherds; those measures were later reversed. The details appear in the memoir of Zhu Bo.

sentences.zh: 唯內史事施行。

translations.literal: Only the Inner Scribe matter was implemented.
translations.idiomatic: Only the reform concerning the inner scribe was actually carried out.

sentences.zh: 多所舉奏，號為煩碎，不稱賢公。

translations.literal: He had many impeachments and memorials; he was called vexatious and minute; he did not match the title worthy lord.
translations.idiomatic: His flood of memorials earned him a reputation for fussiness, and he was not counted among the great statesmen of the day.

sentences.zh: 功名略比薛宣，其材不及也，而經術正直過之。

translations.literal: Merit and fame were roughly comparable to Xue Xuan; his talent did not reach him, but in classical learning and uprightness he surpassed him.
translations.idiomatic: His public career invited comparison with Xue Xuan's, though his administrative gifts fell short; in classical scholarship and moral rigor he was the greater man.

sentences.zh: 武后母在郡，遣吏歸迎。

translations.literal: Wu's stepmother was in the commandery; he sent a clerk to return and welcome her.
translations.idiomatic: His stepmother remained in the commandery, and he sent an official to bring her to the capital.

sentences.zh: 會成帝崩，吏恐道路有盜賊，後母留止，左右或譏武事親不篤。

translations.literal: It happened that Emperor Cheng died; the clerk feared robbers on the roads and had the stepmother remain where she was; some of those around the throne ridiculed Wu for not being devoted in serving his kin.
translations.idiomatic: Emperor Cheng then died; the escort, fearing bandits on the road, left the old lady where she was, and some at court sneered that Wu was neglectful of his family duty.

sentences.zh: 哀帝亦欲改易大臣，遂策免武曰：「君舉錯煩苛，不合眾心，孝聲不聞，惡名流行，無以率示四方。

translations.literal: Emperor Ai also wished to change and replace great ministers; therefore by edict he dismissed Wu saying: "Lord, your measures are vexatious and harsh; they do not accord with the multitude's hearts; sounds of filial piety are not heard; evil names circulate; there is no means thereby to lead and show the four quarters."
translations.idiomatic: Emperor Ai, eager to reshuffle his high ministers, issued an edict dismissing Wu: "Your administration has been harsh and petty, out of step with popular feeling; no one praises your filial conduct, while ill report of you spreads abroad—you cannot be a model to the empire."

sentences.zh: 其上大司空印綬，罷歸就國。」

translations.literal: Submit your Grand Minister of Works seals and ribbons; be dismissed and return to your state."
translations.idiomatic: Surrender the seals of the grand minister of works and retire to your fief."

sentences.zh: 後五歲，諫大夫鮑宣數稱冤之，天子感丞相王嘉之對，而高安侯董賢亦薦武，武由是復徵為御史大夫。

translations.literal: After five years, Grandee Remonstrant Bao Xuan repeatedly called him wronged; the Son of Heaven was moved by Chancellor Wang Jia's responses; also Marquis of Gao'an Dong Xian recommended Wu; Wu for this reason was again summoned to be Imperial Counselor.
translations.idiomatic: Five years later the remonstrant Bao Xuan kept protesting Wu's unjust dismissal; the emperor was swayed by Chancellor Wang Jia's defense of upright men, and the marquis of Gao'an, Dong Xian, also recommended Wu—so Wu was recalled as imperial counselor.

sentences.zh: 月餘，徙為前將軍。

translations.literal: After more than a month, he was transferred to be Forward General.
translations.idiomatic: A little over a month later he was made forward general.

sentences.zh: 先是，新都侯王莽就國，數年，上以太皇太后故徵莽還京師。

translations.literal: Before this, the Marquis of Xindu Wang Mang had gone to his state; after several years, the sovereign because of the Grand Empress Dowager's reason summoned Mang back to the capital.
translations.idiomatic: Earlier, Marquis of Xindu Wang Mang had been sent to his fief; a few years later the emperor recalled him to the capital out of respect for the grand empress dowager.

sentences.zh: 莽從弟成都侯王邑為侍中，矯稱太皇太后指白哀帝，為莽求特進給事中。

translations.literal: Mang's cousin the Marquis of Chengdu Wang Yi was Palace Attendant; falsely he claimed the Grand Empress Dowager's instruction and reported to Emperor Ai, seeking for Mang specially advanced status as Palace Counselor.
translations.idiomatic: Mang's cousin Wang Yi, marquis of Chengdu and a palace attendant, forged a directive from the grand empress dowager and persuaded Emperor Ai to grant Mang the rank of specially advanced with a seat in the palace advisory corps.

sentences.zh: 哀帝復請之，事發覺。

translations.literal: Emperor Ai again requested it; the affair was discovered.
translations.idiomatic: When the emperor repeated the request, the fraud came to light.

sentences.zh: 太后為謝，上以太后故不忍誅之，左遷邑為西河屬國都尉，削千戶。

translations.literal: The Empress Dowager apologized for him; the sovereign because of the Empress Dowager's reason could not bear to execute him; he demoted Yi to be Commandant of Dependent States of Xihe and cut one thousand households.
translations.idiomatic: The empress dowager interceded; unwilling to execute Yi for her sake, the emperor demoted him to commandant of dependent states in Xihe and stripped a thousand households from his fief.

sentences.zh: 後有詔舉大常，莽私從武求舉，武不敢舉。

translations.literal: Afterward there was an edict to recommend the Grand Master of Ceremonies; Mang privately followed Wu seeking recommendation; Wu did not dare recommend.
translations.idiomatic: When an edict called for nominations to the post of grand master of ceremonies, Mang privately asked Wu to name him; Wu refused to comply.

sentences.zh: 後數月，哀帝崩，太后即日引莽入，收大司馬董賢印綬，詔有司舉可大司馬者。

translations.literal: After several months, Emperor Ai died; the Empress Dowager that same day led Mang in, seized Grand Marshal Dong Xian's seals and ribbons, and ordered the responsible offices to recommend who could be Grand Marshal.
translations.idiomatic: Months later Emperor Ai died; the empress dowager at once ushered Mang into the palace, stripped Grand Marshal Dong Xian of his seals, and ordered the ministries to nominate a successor.

sentences.zh: 莽故大司馬，辭位辟丁、傅，眾庶稱以為賢，又太后近親，自大司徒孔光以下舉朝皆舉莽。

translations.literal: Mang was formerly Grand Marshal; he declined position to avoid the Ding and Fu; the multitude praised and deemed him worthy; also he was the Empress Dowager's close kin; from Grand Minister of Education Kong Guang downward the whole court all recommended Mang.
translations.idiomatic: Mang had once served as grand marshal and stepped down to avoid the Ding and Fu factions, which earned him praise as a disinterested man; he was also the empress dowager's kinsman, so from Grand Minister Kong Guang down the entire court nominated him.

sentences.zh: 武為前將軍，素與左將軍公孫祿相善，二人獨謀，以為往時孝惠、孝昭少主之世，外戚呂、霍、上官持權，幾危社稷，今孝成、孝哀比世無嗣，方當選立親近輔幼主，不宜令異姓大臣持權，親疏相錯，為國計便。

translations.literal: Wu was Forward General; he was always on good terms with Left General Gongsun Lu; the two alone plotted, deeming that in former times in the eras of the young lords Emperor Xiaohui and Emperor Xiaozhao, the outer kin Lü, Huo, and Shangguan grasped power and nearly endangered the altars of soil and grain; now Emperor Xiaocheng and Emperor Xiaoai in successive generations had no heirs; just now one ought select and establish the close to assist the young lord; it is not fitting to cause different-surname great ministers to grasp power; close and distant mutually staggered is convenient for the state's plans.
translations.idiomatic: Wu, as forward general, was a longtime ally of Left General Gongsun Lu. The two conferred in private: under Emperors Hui and Zhao, when the throne was held by boys, the consort kin—the Lü, the Huo, the Shangguan—had seized power and nearly overturned the dynasty. Now Cheng and Ai had left no sons in succession; the court must choose near kinsmen to guide a child emperor. Power should not rest with ministers of other surnames; alternating close relatives with more distant ones would serve the state best.

sentences.zh: 於是武舉公孫祿可大司馬，而祿亦舉武。

translations.literal: Thereupon Wu recommended Gongsun Lu as fit for Grand Marshal, and Lu also recommended Wu.
translations.idiomatic: Wu therefore nominated Gongsun Lu for grand marshal, and Lu nominated Wu in return.

sentences.zh: 太后竟自用莽為大司馬。

translations.literal: The Empress Dowager in the end herself employed Mang as Grand Marshal.
translations.idiomatic: The empress dowager overruled them and appointed Mang grand marshal herself.

sentences.zh: 莽風有司劾奏武、公孫祿互相稱舉，皆免。

translations.literal: Mang hinted to the responsible offices to impeach Wu and Gongsun Lu for mutually praising and recommending; both were dismissed.
translations.idiomatic: Mang prompted his officials to impeach Wu and Gongsun Lu for collusive nominations, and both men were removed from office.

sentences.zh: 武就國後，莽寖盛，為宰衡，陰誅不附己者。

translations.literal: After Wu went to his state, Mang gradually grew strong, became Regent and Weigher, and secretly executed those who did not attach to himself.
translations.idiomatic: Once Wu had retired to his fief, Mang's power swelled until he took the title of regent and grand tutor and began quietly eliminating anyone who would not follow him.

sentences.zh: 元始三年，呂寬等事起。

translations.literal: In the third year of Yuanshi, the affair of Lü Kuan and others arose.
translations.idiomatic: In Yuanshi year three the Lü Kuan conspiracy broke.

sentences.zh: 時大司空甄豐承莽風指，遣使者乘傳案治黨與，連引諸所欲誅，上黨鮑宣，南陽彭偉、杜公子，郡國豪桀坐死者數百人。

translations.literal: At the time Grand Minister of Works Zhen Feng received Mang's hinted instructions, sent envoys riding relays to investigate and treat accomplices, serially implicating those he wished to execute—Shangdang Bao Xuan, Nanyang Peng Wei and Du Gongzi—several hundred commandery and kingdom stalwarts died on conviction.
translations.idiomatic: Grand Minister of Works Zhen Feng, doing Mang's bidding, sent couriers racing along the post roads to round up supposed accomplices, dragging in everyone Mang wished dead—among them Bao Xuan of Shangdang, Peng Wei and Master Du of Nanyang—until several hundred local strongmen across the commanderies were executed on trumped-up charges.

sentences.zh: 武在見誣中，大理正檻車徵武，武自殺。

translations.literal: Wu was among those seen as falsely accused; the Chief of the Court of Trials in a caged cart summoned Wu; Wu killed himself.
translations.idiomatic: Wu was caught in that web of false accusation; the chief judge sent a prison cart to fetch him, and Wu took his own life.

sentences.zh: 眾人多冤武者，莽欲厭眾意，令武子況嗣為侯，諡武曰剌侯。

translations.literal: The multitude many deemed Wu wronged; Mang wished to satisfy the multitude's intent; he ordered Wu's son Kuang to succeed as marquis and gave Wu the posthumous title Marquis La.
translations.idiomatic: Public opinion held Wu unjustly ruined; to quiet the outcry Mang let Wu's son Kuang inherit the marquisate and posthumously titled Wu Marquis La (the "piercing" epithet).

sentences.zh: 莽篡位，免況為庶人。

translations.literal: When Mang usurped the throne, he dismissed Kuang to be a commoner.
translations.idiomatic: When Mang seized the throne he reduced Kuang to commoner status.

sentences.zh: 王嘉字公仲，平陵人也。

translations.literal: Wang Jia, styled Gongzhong, was a man of Pingling.
translations.idiomatic: Wang Jia, courtesy name Gongzhong, came from Pingling.

sentences.zh: 以明經射策甲科為郎，坐戶殿門失闌免。

translations.literal: By clarity in the classics and archery-examination in the first class he became a Gentleman; for guarding the hall gate he lost the crossbar and was dismissed.
translations.idiomatic: He entered the court as a gentleman after ranking first on the classics examination, then lost his post for letting the hall gate bar slip while on watch.

sentences.zh: 光祿勳于永除為掾，察廉為南陵丞，復察廉為長陵尉。

translations.literal: Superintendent of the Imperial Household Yu Yong appointed him as aide; on investigation of integrity he became Assistant Magistrate of Nanling; again on investigation of integrity he became Commandant of Changling.
translations.idiomatic: Superintendent Yu Yong took him on as a clerk; twice recommended for integrity, he rose to assistant magistrate of Nanling and then to commandant of Changling.

sentences.zh: 鴻嘉中，舉敦朴能直言，召見宣室，對政事得失，超遷太中大夫。

translations.literal: In the Hongjia period he was recommended as honest and plain and able to speak directly; he was summoned to audience in the Xuanshi Hall and responded on administration's gains and losses; he was specially promoted to Grand Counselor of the Palace.
translations.idiomatic: During Hongjia he was nominated as frank and outspoken, received in the Xuanshi Hall to discuss the strengths and weaknesses of government, and jumped several ranks to grand counselor of the palace.

sentences.zh: 出為九江、河南太守，治甚有聲。

translations.literal: He went out to be Grand Administrator of Jiujiang and Henan; his governing had very great reputation.
translations.idiomatic: As grand administrator of Jiujiang and later of Henan he earned wide renown for his administration.

sentences.zh: 徵入為大鴻臚，徙京兆尹，遷御史大夫。

translations.literal: He was summoned in to be Grand Herald, transferred to Governor of the Capital, promoted to Imperial Counselor.
translations.idiomatic: He was recalled as grand herald, moved to governor of the capital region, and then promoted to imperial counselor.

sentences.zh: 建平三年代平當為丞相，封新甫侯，加食邑千一百戶。

translations.literal: In the third year of Jianping he replaced Ping Dang as Chancellor, was enfeoffed as Marquis of Xinfu, and had added to his fief 1,100 households.
translations.idiomatic: In Jianping year three he succeeded Ping Dang as chancellor, was ennobled as marquis of Xinfu, and received an additional eleven hundred households in his fief.

sentences.zh: 嘉為人剛直嚴毅有威重，上甚敬之。

translations.literal: Jia as a person was firm, straight, stern, and resolute, with awesome weight; the sovereign greatly revered him.
translations.idiomatic: Jia was stern, upright, and carried natural authority; the emperor held him in high regard.

sentences.zh: 哀帝初立，欲匡成帝之政，多所變動，嘉上疏曰：

translations.literal: When Emperor Ai first was established, he wished to rectify Emperor Cheng's government; there were many changes and shifts; Jia submitted a memorial saying:
translations.idiomatic: Early in Emperor Ai's reign, when the court was busy reversing Cheng-era policies, Jia presented a memorial:

sentences.zh: 臣聞聖王之功在於得人。

translations.literal: Your servant has heard that the merit of a sage king lies in obtaining the right men.
translations.idiomatic: Your servant has heard that a sage king's true achievement is winning the right men to serve him.

sentences.zh: 孔子曰：「材難，不其然與！」

translations.literal: Confucius said: "Talent is hard—is it not so!"
translations.idiomatic: Confucius said, "Good men are hard to find—is that not the truth?"

sentences.zh: 「故繼世立諸侯，象賢也。」

translations.literal: "Therefore inheriting the generation and establishing feudal lords is to model the worthy."
translations.idiomatic: "So when a new king enfeoffs the heir among the lords, he is choosing a pattern of virtue."

sentences.zh: 雖不能盡賢，天子為擇臣，立命卿以輔之。

translations.literal: Although they cannot all be worthy, the Son of Heaven for them selects ministers and orders established ministers to assist them.
translations.idiomatic: Even when the heir is not wholly worthy, the Son of Heaven still picks his ministers for him and appoints senior officers to guide him.

sentences.zh: 居是國也，累世尊重，然後士民之眾附焉，是以教化行而治功立。

translations.literal: Dwelling in such a state, generation after generation they are honored and heavy; afterward the multitude of gentlemen and commoners attaches to them; therefore transformation by instruction proceeds and governing merit is established.
translations.idiomatic: Respected across generations in that domain, they win the allegiance of gentry and commoners alike—only then does moral instruction take hold and good government follow.

sentences.zh: 今之郡守重於古諸侯，往者致選賢材，賢材難得，拔擢可用者，或起於囚徒。

translations.literal: Today's commandery governors are weightier than ancient feudal lords; in former times they reached to select worthy talent; worthy talent was hard to obtain; those raised and promoted as usable sometimes rose from prisoners.
translations.idiomatic: A grand administrator today wields more power than a feudal lord of old; past dynasties strained to find able men, and when talent ran short they did not hesitate to promote a man straight from chains.

sentences.zh: 昔魏尚坐事繫，文帝感馮唐之言，遣使持節赦其罪，拜為雲中太守，匈奴忌之。

translations.literal: In olden times Wei Shang sat in affair and was bound; Emperor Wen was moved by Feng Tang's words, sent an envoy bearing staff to pardon his crime, appointed him Grand Administrator of Yunzhong, and the Xiongnu feared him.
translations.idiomatic: Wei Shang was once jailed for an offense; Emperor Wen, persuaded by Feng Tang, sent an envoy with the imperial staff to free him and made him grand administrator of Yunzhong, to the Xiongnu' alarm.

sentences.zh: 武帝擢韓安國於徒中，拜為梁內史，骨肉以安。

translations.literal: Emperor Wu raised Han Anguo from among convicts, appointed him Inner Scribe of Liang, and flesh and bone thereby were pacified.
translations.idiomatic: Emperor Wu pulled Han Anguo out of penal labor and named him inner scribe of Liang, and the imperial house knew peace again.

sentences.zh: 張敞為京兆尹，有罪當免，黠吏知而犯敞，敞收殺之，其家自冤，使者覆獄，劾敞賊殺人，上逮捕不下，會免，亡命數十日，宣帝徵敞拜為冀州刺史，卒獲其用。

translations.literal: Zhang Chang was Governor of the Capital; he had guilt and ought to have been dismissed; a cunning clerk knew and offended Chang; Chang seized and killed him; his household themselves called it wrong; an envoy reviewed the prison case and impeached Chang for bandit murder; the sovereign ordered arrest but did not carry it down; it happened he was dismissed; he fled his mandate several tens of days; Emperor Xuan summoned Chang and appointed him Regional Inspector of Ji; in the end his use was obtained.
translations.idiomatic: Zhang Chang, as governor of the capital, faced removal for an offense; a wily subordinate deliberately provoked him, and Chang had the man executed. The family cried foul; an imperial investigator reheard the case and charged Chang with malicious killing. The emperor withheld the warrant for his arrest until Chang was out of office; Chang then fled and hid for some weeks until Emperor Xuan recalled him as regional inspector of Ji—where he proved indispensable.

sentences.zh: 前世非私此三人，貪其材器有益於公家也。

translations.literal: Former generations did not privately favor these three men; they coveted their talents and implements as beneficial to the public house.
translations.idiomatic: Those earlier courts did not show those three men personal favor; they valued their abilities because they served the state.

sentences.zh: 孝文時，吏居官者或長子孫，以官為氏，倉氏、庫氏則倉庫吏之後也。

translations.literal: In the time of Emperor Xiaowen, among clerks dwelling in office some grew old and had sons and grandsons; they took office as surname; the Cang clan and Ku clan were afterward of granary and treasury clerks.
translations.idiomatic: Under Emperor Wen some families held the same minor post for generations until the office became their surname—the Cangs and the Kus, for instance, descend from granary and treasury clerks.

sentences.zh: 其二千石長吏亦安官樂職，然後上下相望，莫有苟且之意。

translations.literal: Thereupon those two-thousand-dan senior officials also were secure in office and pleased with their posts; afterward superiors and inferiors looked to one another; none had a mind of careless expediency.
translations.idiomatic: Then the two-thousand-dan men could settle into their posts with confidence; ruler and officials looked to each other in good faith, and no one nursed a merely cynical careerism.

sentences.zh: 其後稍稍變易，公卿以下傳相促急，又數改更政事，司隸、部刺史察過悉劾，發揚陰私，吏或居官數月而退，送故迎新，交錯道路。

translations.literal: Afterward gradually there were changes; from dukes and ministers downward they passed on mutual urgency; also repeatedly they altered government affairs; the Metropolitan Superintendent and Regional Inspectors investigated faults and all impeached, exposing and raising hidden private matters; clerks sometimes dwelt in office several months then withdrew; escorting the old and welcoming the new crisscrossed the roads.
translations.idiomatic: Little by little the climate changed: from the highest ministers down, everyone hectored everyone else; policy swung back and forth; metropolitan and regional inspectors hunted for slip-ups and dragged private faults into the open; magistrates lasted only months before transfer; processions seeing off the outgoing and greeting the incoming choked the highways.

sentences.zh: 中材苟容求全，下材懷危內顧，壹切營私者多。

translations.literal: Those of middling talent carelessly accommodated to seek completeness; those of lower talent harbored peril and looked inward; in all cases those who schemed for private gain were many.
translations.idiomatic: Mediocre men played it safe to survive; lesser men watched their backs; across the board, self-dealing multiplied.

sentences.zh: 二千石益輕賤，吏民慢易之。

translations.literal: The two-thousand-dan grew ever more lightly despised; clerks and commoners treated them with contempt.
translations.idiomatic: Grand administrators lost standing until clerks and commoners openly treated them with disrespect.

sentences.zh: 或持其微過，增加成罪，言於刺史、司隸，或至上書章下；

translations.literal: Some seized their slight faults, increased and made them into crimes, spoke to the Regional Inspector and Metropolitan Superintendent, or even reached to submitting memorials and documents descending;
translations.idiomatic: People seized on petty faults, inflated them into crimes, whispered to regional and metropolitan inspectors, or pushed memorials to the throne until rescripts came down against the victim.

sentences.zh: 眾庶知其易危，小失意則有離畔之心。

translations.literal: The multitude knew they were easily endangered; on small loss of favor there was a mind of separation and rebellion.
translations.idiomatic: The people saw how precarious high office had become; the slightest setback could turn a man disloyal.

sentences.zh: 前山陽亡徒蘇令等從橫，吏士臨難，莫肯伏節死義，以守相威權素奪也。

translations.literal: Formerly the Shanyang escaped convict Su Ling and others ranged horizontally and vertically; clerks and soldiers facing peril none were willing to submit to the tally and die for righteousness—because the governors' and chancellors' awesome authority had long been stripped away.
translations.idiomatic: When Su Ling the escaped convict from Shanyang and his band rampaged, not one official or soldier would stand firm and die for duty—because governors and chancellors had long since been stripped of real authority.

sentences.zh: 孝成皇帝悔之，下詔書，二千石不為縱，遣使者賜金，尉厚其意，誠以為國家有急，取辦於二千石，二千石尊重難危，乃能使下。

translations.literal: Emperor Xiaocheng regretted it; he issued an edict that two-thousand-dan were not to be charged with the crime of deliberate laxity in office; he sent envoys to bestow gold and comfort their intent, truly deeming that when the state had urgency, it took results from the two-thousand-dan; when two-thousand-dan were honored, weighty, and hard to endanger, then they could employ those below.
translations.idiomatic: Emperor Cheng regretted the policy: he promulgated an edict shielding grand administrators from trumped-up charges of abetting criminals, sent envoys with gold to steady their morale, and declared that in an emergency the realm must rely on those officers—only when they stand secure in dignity and danger alike can they truly lead their subordinates.

sentences.zh: 孝宣皇帝愛其良民吏，有章劾，事留中，會赦壹解。

translations.literal: Emperor Xiaoxuan loved his good commoner clerks; when there was a memorial impeaching, the matter was detained in the palace; meeting a general amnesty it was once dissolved.
translations.idiomatic: Emperor Xuan cherished honest local officials: impeachment papers were held inside the palace until a general amnesty could clear the matter in one stroke.

sentences.zh: 故事，尚書希下章，為煩擾百姓，證驗繫治，或死獄中，章文必有「敢告之」字乃下。

translations.literal: By precedent, the Masters of Writing rarely sent down documents, because it vexed and disturbed the hundred surnames, evidence and verification bound and treated, some dying in prison; the memorial text had to have the words "dare to report it" before it was sent down.
translations.idiomatic: By established rule the secretariat seldom forwarded accusations to the provinces, lest common folk be harrowed by endless questioning and jailings—even deaths in custody; a charge had to carry the formula "I dare report" before it went out.

sentences.zh: 唯陛下留神於擇賢，記善忘過，容忍臣子，勿責以備。

translations.literal: Only may Your Majesty fix attention on selecting the worthy, record good and forget faults, tolerate and bear with ministers and sons, do not require them with completeness.
translations.idiomatic: May Your Majesty devote care to choosing able men, remember their strengths and overlook small faults, bear with your servants, and not demand perfection of everyone.

sentences.zh: 二千石、部刺史、三輔縣令有材任職者，人情不能不有過差，宜可闊略，令盡力者有所勸。

translations.literal: Among two-thousand-dan, Regional Inspectors, and capital-district county magistrates who have talent and hold office, human feeling cannot fail to have faults and slips; it is fitting to be broad and omitting, so that those who exhaust strength have something to encourage them.
translations.idiomatic: Grand administrators, regional inspectors, and magistrates of the capital counties who are genuinely competent will still stumble now and then; Your Majesty should judge them generously so that men who give their all still have reason to try harder.

sentences.zh: 此方今急務，國家之利也。

translations.literal: This is the present urgent task; it is the benefit of the state.
translations.idiomatic: That is the pressing business of the moment and the true interest of the realm.

sentences.zh: 前蘇令發，欲遣大夫使逐問狀，時見大夫無可使者，召盩厔令尹逢拜為諫大夫遣之。

translations.literal: Formerly when Su Ling arose, they wished to dispatch a grandee envoy to pursue and inquire the situation; at the time they saw among grandees none who could be sent; they summoned Zhizhi Magistrate Yin Feng, appointed him Grandee Remonstrant, and dispatched him.
translations.idiomatic: When Su Ling's revolt broke out the court wanted a senior envoy to investigate, but no suitable grandee could be found—so the emperor summoned Yin Feng, magistrate of Zhizhi, named him grandee remonstrant, and sent him.

sentences.zh: 令諸大夫有材能者甚少，宜豫畜養可成就者，則士赴難不愛其死；

translations.literal: Now among the various grandees those who have talent and ability are very few; it is fitting beforehand to store and nurture those who can be brought to completion; then gentlemen facing hardship will not spare their deaths;
translations.idiomatic: Capable grandees are scarce today; the court should cultivate promising men in advance, so that when crisis comes gentlemen will go to their deaths without hesitation.

sentences.zh: 臨事倉卒乃求，非所以明朝廷也。

translations.literal: Facing affairs in haste and then seeking is not the means whereby to clarify the court.
translations.idiomatic: Scrambling for talent only after trouble erupts is no way to show the world that this court knows its business.

sentences.zh: 嘉因薦儒者公孫光、滿昌及能吏蕭咸、薛修等，皆故二千石有名稱。

translations.literal: Jia thereupon recommended the Ru scholars Gongsun Guang and Man Chang and the able clerks Xiao Xian, Xue Xiu, and others; all were former two-thousand-dan with reputation and title.
translations.idiomatic: Jia therefore nominated the scholars Gongsun Guang and Man Chang and the seasoned administrators Xiao Xian and Xue Xiu—all former grand administrators of proven reputation.

sentences.zh: 天子納而用之。

translations.literal: The Son of Heaven accepted and employed them.
translations.idiomatic: The emperor accepted the nominations and appointed them.

sentences.zh: 後數月，日食，舉直言，嘉復奏封事曰：

translations.literal: After several months there was a solar eclipse; they raised those who speak directly; Jia again submitted a sealed document saying:
translations.idiomatic: Months later an eclipse prompted a call for frank counsel; Jia again submitted a sealed memorial:

sentences.zh: 臣聞咎繇戒帝舜曰：「亡敖佚欲有國，兢兢業業，一日二日萬機。」

translations.literal: Your servant has heard that Gao Yao warned Emperor Shun, saying: "Do not be arrogant or indulge desires and possess a state; be fearful and fearful; in one day, two days, the myriad threads of affairs."
translations.idiomatic: Your servant recalls Gao Yao's warning to Shun: "Do not grow arrogant or wallow in pleasure while you hold the realm; tremble with care—day and night the myriad threads of government pull at you."

sentences.zh: 箕子戒武王曰：「臣無有作威作福，亡有玉食；

translations.literal: Jizi warned King Wu, saying: "Ministers must not wield arbitrary power nor enjoy jade-vessel feasts;
translations.idiomatic: Jizi warned King Wu: "No minister may arrogate the power of reward and punishment, nor dine with the regalia of a sovereign;"

sentences.zh: 臣之有作威作福玉食，害于而家，凶于而國，人用側頗辟，民用僭慝。」

translations.literal: if ministers wield arbitrary power and enjoy jade-vessel feasts, it harms your house and brings ill to your state; officials grow slanted and perverse; the people grow lawless and depraved."
translations.idiomatic: when ministers seize such power and live like kings, your house suffers harm and your state meets ruin; officials grow crooked and the people turn lawless."

sentences.zh: 言如此則逆尊卑之序，亂陰陽之統，而害及王者，其國極危。

translations.literal: The meaning is that thus one reverses the order of honored and humble, disorders the alignment of yin and yang, and harm reaches the king; his state is extremely perilous.
translations.idiomatic: That is to say: such conduct overturns proper rank, throws yin and yang out of balance, and the harm reaches the throne itself—the state stands at the brink.

sentences.zh: 國人傾仄不正，民用僭差不壹，此君不由法度，上下失序之敗也。

translations.literal: The people of the state lean awry and are not straight; the people use overstepping and slips and are not unified—this is the defeat of a lord who does not follow law and measure, superiors and inferiors losing order.
translations.idiomatic: When the elite lean every which way and common folk no longer know their place, the fault lies with a ruler who ignores the statutes—high and low have lost their proper order.

sentences.zh: 武王躬履此道，隆至成康。

translations.literal: King Wu personally trod this way; it flourished reaching Cheng and Kang.
translations.idiomatic: King Wu walked that path himself, and its blessings carried all the way to the peace of Cheng and Kang.

sentences.zh: 自是以後，縱心恣欲，法度陵遲，至於臣弒君，子弒父。

translations.literal: From this afterward, they let loose heart and indulged desires; law and measure declined and slowed, reaching to ministers assassinating lords, sons assassinating fathers.
translations.idiomatic: Afterward rulers followed their whims while the laws rotted away, until ministers murdered their sovereigns and sons murdered their fathers.

sentences.zh: 父子至親，失禮患生，何況異姓之臣？

translations.literal: Father and son are utmost kin; lose ritual and calamity is born—how much more different-surname ministers?
translations.idiomatic: Even the bond of father and son, the closest tie of all, breaks down when ritual fails—how much more easily, then, the loyalty of unrelated ministers?

sentences.zh: 孔子曰：「道千乘之國，敬事而信，節用而愛人，使民以時。」

translations.literal: Confucius said: "To lead a state of a thousand chariots: be reverent in affairs and trustworthy, restrain expenditure and cherish men, employ the people according to the seasons."
translations.idiomatic: Confucius said, "To govern a state that can field a thousand chariots: handle public business with reverence and good faith, spend frugally and care for your people, and call the commoners to labor only in season."

sentences.zh: 孝文皇帝備行此道，海內蒙恩，為漢太宗。

translations.literal: Emperor Xiaowen fully practiced this way; within the seas they received grace; he became Han's Taizong.
translations.idiomatic: Emperor Wen embodied that teaching; the whole empire received his kindness, and posterity honors him as the Grand Exemplar of the Han.

sentences.zh: 孝宣皇帝賞罰信明，施與有節，記人之功，忽於小過，以致治平。

translations.literal: Emperor Xiaoxuan in rewards and punishments was trustworthy and clear; in grants and gifts there was moderation; he recorded men's merits and overlooked small faults, thereby achieving order and peace.
translations.idiomatic: Emperor Xuan made rewards and penalties plain and predictable, spent with restraint, remembered men's achievements, and overlooked petty slips—thus he brought the realm to stability.

sentences.zh: 孝元皇帝奉承大業，溫恭少欲，都內錢四十萬萬，水衡錢二十五萬萬，少府錢十八萬萬。

translations.literal: Emperor Xiaoyuan inherited the great enterprise; he was warm, reverent, and had few desires; in the capital granaries cash was forty hundred million; in the Aqueduct Treasury twenty-five hundred million; in the Privy Treasury eighteen hundred million.
translations.idiomatic: Emperor Yuan took up the great legacy in gentleness and restraint: the capital treasuries held four billion cash in the imperial storehouse, two and a half billion in the hydraulic treasury, and one point eight billion in the privy purse.

sentences.zh: 嘗幸上林，後宮馮貴人從臨獸圈，猛獸驚出，貴人前當之，元帝嘉美其義，賜錢五萬。

translations.literal: Once he favored Shanglin; a rear-palace Honored Person Feng followed to view the beast pen; a fierce beast startled and came out; the Honored Person stepped before it to block; Emperor Yuan praised her righteousness and bestowed fifty thousand cash.
translations.idiomatic: Once at the Shanglin park the concubine Feng of honorable rank stepped between the emperor and a maddened beast; Yuan admired her courage and gave her fifty thousand cash.

sentences.zh: 掖庭見親，有加賞賜，屬其人勿眾謝。

translations.literal: When the lateral courts met kin, there were added grants and gifts; he charged those persons not to thank in a crowd.
translations.idiomatic: When palace women received kin, any extra bounty came with orders not to parade thanks before the whole court.

sentences.zh: 示平惡偏，重失人心，賞賜節約。

translations.literal: To show leveling and hate partiality, to value not losing men's hearts, grants and gifts were restrained and economical.
translations.idiomatic: He meant to show even-handed justice, avoid favoritism, keep popular confidence, and keep largesse within bounds.

sentences.zh: 是時外戚貲千萬者少耳，故少府水衡見錢多也。

translations.literal: At this time among outer kin those whose property was ten million were few; therefore in the Privy Treasury and Aqueduct Treasury visible cash was much.
translations.idiomatic: Few consort families then counted their wealth in the tens of millions—hence the privy and hydraulic treasuries stayed flush.

sentences.zh: 雖遭初元、永光凶年飢饉，加有西羌之變，外奉師旅，內振貧民，終無傾危之憂，以府臧內充實也。

translations.literal: Although they encountered the Chuyuan and Yongguang ill years of famine, added to there was the Western Qiang uprising; outwardly they supplied armies; inwardly they relieved the poor; in the end there was no fear of overturning peril—because the treasuries within were full and solid.
translations.idiomatic: Even through the bad harvests of Chuyuan and Yongguang and the Qiang revolt in the west—armies in the field and relief for the starving at home—the dynasty never teetered, because the granaries and treasuries remained full.

sentences.zh: 孝成皇帝時，諫臣多言燕出之害，及女寵專愛，耽於酒色，損德傷年，其言甚切，然終不怨怒也。

translations.literal: In the time of Emperor Xiaocheng, remonstrating ministers many spoke of the harm of swallow outings and of women's favor monopolizing love, sinking in wine and sex, injuring virtue and harming years; their words were very cutting, yet to the end he was not resentful or angry.
translations.idiomatic: Under Emperor Cheng remonstrators often warned of the evils of secret excursions, of a favorite's monopoly on affection, of dissipation in wine and women that sapped virtue and shortened life; their language was blunt, yet the emperor never turned bitter on them.

sentences.zh: 寵臣淳于長、張放、史育，育數貶退，家貲不滿千萬，放斥逐就國，長榜死於獄。

translations.literal: Favored ministers Chunyu Chang, Zhang Fang, and Shi Yu—Yu was repeatedly demoted and retreated; household property did not reach ten million; Fang was expelled and sent to his state; Chang was beaten to death in prison.
translations.idiomatic: Among Cheng's favorites—Chunyu Chang, Zhang Fang, and Shi Yu—Shi Yu was demoted time and again and never amassed ten million in family wealth; Zhang Fang was banished to his fief; Chunyu Chang died under the flogging staff in jail.

sentences.zh: 不以私愛害公義，故雖多內譏，朝廷安平，傳業陛下。

translations.literal: He did not by private love harm public righteousness; therefore although there was much inner ridicule, the court was secure and peaceful and transmitted the enterprise to Your Majesty.
translations.idiomatic: Because he never let private affection corrupt public duty, the court stayed stable despite palace gossip, and the mandate passed down to Your Majesty.

sentences.zh: 陛下在國之時，好詩書，上儉節，徵來所過道上稱誦德美，此天下所以回心也。

translations.literal: When Your Majesty was in your state, you loved the Odes and Documents and esteemed frugality; on the roads along which you were summoned people praised your virtue and beauty; this is why all under Heaven turned their hearts.
translations.idiomatic: While still heir-apparent you loved the classics and honored simplicity; everywhere your procession passed the people praised your virtue—that is why the realm put its hope in you.

sentences.zh: 初即位，易帷帳，去錦繡，乘輿席緣綈繒而已。

translations.literal: When first ascending the throne, you changed curtains and canopies, removed brocade and embroidery; the carriage mat's border was coarse silk only.
translations.idiomatic: At your accession you replaced palace hangings, stripped out brocade, and rode with mat borders of plain damask—nothing more.

sentences.zh: 共皇寢廟比比當作，憂閔元元，惟用度不足，以義割恩，輒且止息，今始作治。

translations.literal: The Gong Emperor's tomb-shrines repeatedly were due to be built; you grieved and pitied the black-headed ones; only because expenditure was insufficient, by righteousness you cut affection and always for the moment stopped and rested; now they begin construction and repair.
translations.idiomatic: Your father's mausoleum shrines should have risen one after another, yet out of pity for the common people and fear of the treasury you set duty above affection and halted the work—only now has building slowly resumed.

sentences.zh: 而駙馬都尉董賢亦起官寺上林中，又為賢治大第，開門鄉北闕，引王渠灌園池，使者護作，賞賜吏卒，甚於治宗廟。

translations.literal: Yet the Commandant of Equipage Dong Xian also raised official buildings in Shanglin; also for Xian they built a great mansion, opening the gate facing the northern gate-tower, drawing the Wang canal to irrigate garden pools; envoys supervised the work; grants to clerks and soldiers exceeded those for building the ancestral temples.
translations.idiomatic: Meanwhile Commandant Dong Xian has had yamen offices raised inside Shanglin Park, and a mansion for him whose gate lines up with the northern palace tower, with the imperial canal diverted to fill his gardens—imperial couriers oversee the labor, and the gratuities to workmen outstrip what was spent on the imperial shrines.

sentences.zh: 賢母病，長安廚給祠具，道中過者皆飲食。

translations.literal: When Xian's mother was ill, the Chang'an kitchens supplied sacrificial implements; those who passed on the road all received drink and food.
translations.idiomatic: When Dong Xian's mother fell ill, the capital kitchens supplied ritual vessels for her prayers, and every traveler on the road was fed from the public purse.

sentences.zh: 為賢治器，器成，奏御乃行，或物好，特賜其工，自貢獻宗廟三宮，猶不至此。

translations.literal: For Xian they made vessels; when vessels were complete, they memorialized the sovereign and only then proceeded; if the thing was fine, they specially bestowed on the craftsman—even from tribute presented to the ancestral temples and the three palaces, still it did not reach this.
translations.idiomatic: Vessels forged for Dong Xian must be shown to the throne before use; if a piece is especially fine the artisan receives a personal reward—treatment stricter than what attends tribute for the ancestral shrines and the three palaces.

sentences.zh: 賢家有賓婚及見親，諸官並共，賜及倉頭奴婢，人十萬錢。

translations.literal: When Xian's household had guest weddings or met kin, the various offices jointly supplied; grants reached to young male slaves and maidservants, each person one hundred thousand cash.
translations.idiomatic: Whenever Dong Xian entertained wedding guests or received relatives, every ministry contributed, down to a hundred thousand cash for each of his young male slaves and maidservants.

sentences.zh: 使者護視，發取市物，百賈震動，道路讙譁，群臣惶惑。

translations.literal: Envoys supervised and watched; they issued and took market goods; the hundred merchants trembled; on the roads there was clamor; the host of ministers was frightened and confused.
translations.idiomatic: Imperial agents supervise the scene, requisitioning goods from the markets; merchants panic, the streets buzz with rumor, and the ministers walk in a daze.

sentences.zh: 詔書罷菀，而以賜賢二千餘頃，均田之制從此墮壞。

translations.literal: An edict document abolished the park, yet gave to Xian more than two thousand qing; the equal-field system from this collapsed and broke.
translations.idiomatic: An edict closed the imperial breeding park—only to hand more than two thousand qing of it to Dong Xian, wrecking the equitable land system at a stroke.

sentences.zh: 奢僭放縱，變亂陰陽，災異眾多，百姓訛言，持籌相驚，被髮徒跣而走，乘馬者馳，天惑其意，不能自止。

translations.literal: Extravagance and usurpation, release and indulgence, changed and disordered yin and yang; portents were many; the hundred surnames had false rumors; holding tallies they alarmed one another; disheveled hair, barefoot they ran; those riding horses galloped; Heaven confused their intent; they could not stop themselves.
translations.idiomatic: Such luxury and presumption have thrown yin and yang out of joint: omens multiply, wild rumors fly, and folk run through the streets barefoot with hair unbound, tally-sticks in hand, while horsemen gallop past—Heaven has muddled their minds and they cannot stop.

sentences.zh: 或以為籌者策失之戒也。

translations.literal: Some thought that the tallies were a warning of policy slips.
translations.idiomatic: Some read those counting-rods as Heaven's warning that the court's policies had gone astray.

sentences.zh: 陛下素仁智慎事，今而有此大譏。

translations.literal: Your Majesty has always been humane, wise, and careful in affairs; now yet there is this great ridicule.
translations.idiomatic: You have always been humane, wise, and careful—yet now the world heaps this bitter mockery on you.

sentences.zh: 孔子曰：「危而不持，顛而不扶，則將安用彼相矣！」

translations.literal: Confucius said: "When there is peril yet one does not hold, when there is overturning yet one does not support—then what use will there be for that minister!"
translations.idiomatic: Confucius said, "If in danger you do not lend a hand, if at the brink you offer no support—what good is such a minister?"

sentences.zh: 臣嘉幸得備位，竊內悲傷不能通愚忠之信；

translations.literal: Your servant Jia has fortunately obtained to fill a place; secretly within he grieves and sorrows, unable to communicate the trust of foolish loyalty;
translations.idiomatic: Your servant Jia is honored to hold office, yet grieves that he cannot make his stubborn loyalty understood.

sentences.zh: 身死有益於國，不敢自惜。

translations.literal: If body death benefits the state, he does not dare spare himself.
translations.idiomatic: If my death would serve the realm, I would not cling to life.

sentences.zh: 唯陛下慎己之所獨鄉，察眾人之所共疑。

translations.literal: Only may Your Majesty be careful in what you alone incline to, investigate what the multitude together doubts.
translations.idiomatic: May Your Majesty weigh the private inclinations you alone pursue against the doubts the whole court shares.

sentences.zh: 往者寵臣鄧通、韓嫣驕貴失度，逸豫無厭，小人不勝情欲，卒陷罪辜。

translations.literal: In former times favored ministers Deng Tong and Han Yan were arrogant and noble beyond measure, leisurely and at ease without satiety; petty men could not overcome emotion and desire; in the end they fell into crime and guilt.
translations.idiomatic: The favorites Deng Tong and Han Yan rose too high, gave themselves to pleasure without limit, and as petty men could not master passion—both ended in crime and ruin.

sentences.zh: 亂國亡驅，不終其祿，所謂愛之適足以害之者也。

translations.literal: They disordered the state and destroyed their persons; they did not complete their emoluments—what is called loving them was just sufficient to harm them.
translations.idiomatic: They brought chaos on the state and destruction on themselves, never outliving their stipends—proof that doting on a favorite can be the surest way to destroy him.

sentences.zh: 宜深覽前世，以節賢↨，全安其命。

translations.literal: It is fitting deeply to survey former generations, thereby to restrain private favor toward the favored, and wholly secure his life.
translations.idiomatic: Ponder the precedents of earlier ages, curb the excess of royal affection toward a favorite, and so preserve his life intact.

sentences.zh: 於是上寖不說，而愈愛賢，不能自勝。

translations.literal: Thereupon the sovereign gradually was not pleased, yet the more he loved Xian and could not overcome himself.
translations.idiomatic: The emperor grew steadily colder toward Jia's counsel yet more infatuated with Dong Xian—utterly unable to master himself.

sentences.zh: 會祖母傅太后薨，上因託傅太后遺詔，令成帝母王太后下丞相御史，益封賢二千戶，及賜孔鄉侯、汝昌侯、陽新侯國。

translations.literal: It happened that the Grand Empress Dowager Fu died; the sovereign thereupon relied on Empress Dowager Fu's testamentary edict, ordering Emperor Cheng's mother Empress Dowager Wang to send down to the Chancellor and Imperial Counselor to add to Xian's fief two thousand households, and to grant the Marquis of Kongxiang, Marquis of Ruchang, and Marquis of Yangxin states.
translations.idiomatic: When the grand empress dowager Fu died, the emperor claimed to act on her deathbed will and had Empress Dowager Wang instruct the chancellor and counselor to add two thousand households to Dong Xian's income and to enlarge the fiefs of the marquises of Kongxiang, Ruchang, and Yangxin.

sentences.zh: 嘉封還詔書，因奏封事諫上及太后曰：「臣聞爵祿土地，天之有也。

translations.literal: Jia sealed and returned the edict document; thereupon he submitted a sealed memorial remonstrating the sovereign and the Empress Dowager, saying: "Your servant has heard that ranks, emoluments, and land are what Heaven possesses.
translations.idiomatic: Jia resealed the edict and returned it unopened, then sent a sealed memorial to the emperor and empress dowager: "Your servant has heard that noble rank, salary, and territory belong to Heaven.

sentences.zh: 《書》云：『天命有德，五服五章哉！』

translations.literal: The Documents says: 'Heaven orders the virtuous; the five garments have five emblems!'
translations.idiomatic: The Book of Documents says, 'Heaven appoints the virtuous; the five grades of robe mark the five ranks of honor.'

sentences.zh: 王者代天爵人，尤宜慎之。

translations.literal: The king acts for Heaven in enfeoffing men; especially one ought to be careful therein.
translations.idiomatic: The Son of Heaven ennobles men on Heaven's behalf; he must proceed with the utmost care.

sentences.zh: 裂地而封，不得其宜，則眾庶不服，感動陰陽，其害疾自深。

translations.literal: Splitting land and enfeoffing, if one does not obtain the right fit, then the multitude will not submit; it stirs and moves yin and yang; its harm is swift and itself deep.
translations.idiomatic: Ill-judged enfeoffments alienate the people, disturb the balance of yin and yang, and bring harm with terrible speed.

sentences.zh: 今聖體久不平，此臣嘉所內懼也。

translations.literal: Now the sage body long has not been at peace; this is what your servant Jia inwardly fears.
translations.idiomatic: Your Majesty's health has long been unsettled—that is what fills your servant with dread.

sentences.zh: 高安侯賢，佞幸之臣，陛下傾爵位以貴之，單貨財以富之，損至尊以寵之，主威已黜，府藏已竭，唯恐不足。

translations.literal: Marquis of Gao'an Xian is a sycophant-favored minister; Your Majesty tilts ranks and positions to honor him, exhausts goods and wealth to enrich him, injures the utmost veneration to favor him; the lord's awe already is dimmed; treasuries already are exhausted; he fears only that it is not enough.
translations.idiomatic: Marquis of Gao'an Dong Xian is a creature of flattery: you have tilted the whole ladder of rank to exalt him, poured out the treasury to enrich him, and bent the majesty of the throne to indulge him—sovereign dignity is eclipsed, the storehouses are bare, and still you fear it is not enough.

sentences.zh: 財皆民力所為，孝文皇帝欲起露臺，重百金之費，克己不作。

translations.literal: Wealth is all what the people's strength accomplishes; Emperor Xiaowen wished to raise an open terrace; he valued the expense of a hundred jin of gold and restrained himself and did not build.
translations.idiomatic: Every coin comes from the people's labor: Emperor Wen once planned an open terrace, balked at a cost of a hundred catties of gold, and gave it up.

sentences.zh: 今賢散公賦以施私惠，一家至受千金，往古以來貴臣未嘗有此，流聞四方，皆同怨之。

translations.literal: Now Xian scatters public levies to apply private grace; one household reaches receiving a thousand jin of gold; from ancient times onward noble ministers have never had this; flowing report reaches the four quarters; all alike resent him.
translations.idiomatic: Now Dong Xian spends the public revenue on private largesse; a single household pockets a thousand pounds in gold—no favorite in history has been so enriched, and resentment runs through the four quarters.

sentences.zh: 里諺曰：『千人所指，無病而死。』

translations.literal: A village proverb says: 'When a thousand men point, without sickness one dies.'
translations.idiomatic: The proverb runs, 'Let a thousand fingers point at a man and he will die though no illness touch him.'

sentences.zh: 臣常為之寒心。

translations.literal: Your servant constantly for this feels cold in the heart.
translations.idiomatic: Your servant shudders whenever he thinks of it.

sentences.zh: 今太皇太后以永信太后遺詔，詔丞相御史益賢戶，賜三侯國，臣嘉竊惑。

translations.literal: Now the Grand Empress Dowager by Empress Dowager Yongxin's testamentary edict ordered the Chancellor and Imperial Counselor to increase Xian's households and grant the three marquises states—your servant Jia secretly is perplexed.
translations.idiomatic: Now the grand empress dowager invokes a testament of the late empress dowager of Yongxin and orders the chancellor and counselor to swell Dong Xian's fief and add whole principalities for three marquises—your servant is baffled.

sentences.zh: 山崩地動，日食於三朝，皆陰侵陽之戒也。

translations.literal: Mountains collapsed and earth shook; there was solar eclipse on three mornings—all are warnings of yin encroaching on yang.
translations.idiomatic: Landslides, earthquakes, and eclipses on three successive mornings—all are Heaven's warning that yin is overwhelming yang.

sentences.zh: 前賢已再封，晏、商再易邑，業緣私橫求，恩已過厚，求索自恣，不知厭足，甚傷尊卑之義，不可以示天下，為害痛矣！

translations.literal: Formerly Xian was already twice enfeoffed; Yan and Shang twice had their fiefs changed; relying on private connections they boldly sought; grace already passed thick; seeking and demanding self-indulgent, not knowing satiety—greatly injuring the meaning of honored and humble; it cannot be shown to all under Heaven; the harm is bitter indeed!
translations.idiomatic: Dong Xian has already been enfeoffed twice; Ding Yan and Ding Shang have had their fiefs reshuffled again and again; through private connections they keep pressing for more—favor heaped past measure, greed still unsated, until the whole hierarchy of rank is mocked. You cannot display such conduct to the empire; the harm is cruel indeed.

sentences.zh: 臣驕侵罔，陰陽失節，氣感相動，害及身體。

translations.literal: Ministers are arrogant, encroaching, and deceive; yin and yang lose their seasons; vapors mutually stir and move; harm reaches the body.
translations.idiomatic: When ministers grow arrogant and deceitful, yin and yang fall out of season; the vital ethers clash and the damage reaches Your Majesty's own person.

sentences.zh: 陛下寢疾久不平，繼嗣未立，宜思正萬事，順天人之心，以求福祐，柰何輕身肆意，不念高祖之勤苦垂立制度欲傳之於無窮哉！

translations.literal: Your Majesty has long been ill without recovery; the heir-apparent is not yet established; it is fitting to think of rectifying the myriad affairs, complying with Heaven and men's hearts, thereby to seek blessing and aid—how then can you lightly treat your person and indulge your desires, not recalling Gaozu's toil in establishing institutions he meant to hand down without end!
translations.idiomatic: Your illness lingers, the heir yet unchosen: you should set the myriad affairs aright and win the hearts of Heaven and men—yet you risk your person and follow every whim, forgetting how Gaozu toiled to frame institutions meant to endure forever.

sentences.zh: 孝經曰：『天子有爭臣七人，雖無道，不失其天下。』

translations.literal: The Classic of Filial Piety says: 'The Son of Heaven has seven ministers who contend; though without the Way, he does not lose his all under Heaven.'
translations.idiomatic: The Classic of Filial Piety says, 'If a Son of Heaven keeps seven ministers who will speak bluntly, he will not lose the realm even when his own conduct falters.'

sentences.zh: 臣謹封上詔書，不敢露見，非愛死而不自法，恐天下聞之，故不敢自劾。

translations.literal: Your servant respectfully sealed and submitted the edict document; he did not dare expose and show it; it is not that he loves death and does not impeach himself—he feared all under Heaven would hear it; therefore he did not dare impeach himself.
translations.idiomatic: Your servant returns the edict sealed because he dares not publish it—not from cowardice about impeaching himself, but lest the empire learn that the chancellor had to denounce his own sovereign's order.

sentences.zh: 愚贛數犯忌諱，唯陛下省察。」

translations.literal: Foolish and blunt, repeatedly offending taboos—only may Your Majesty examine and consider."
translations.idiomatic: A dull man has again trespassed on forbidden ground—may Your Majesty judge him with care.

sentences.zh: 初，廷尉梁相與丞相長史、御史中丞及五二千石雜治東平王雲獄，時冬月未盡二旬，而相心疑雲冤，獄有飾辭，奏欲傳之長安，更下公卿覆治。

translations.literal: At the beginning, Commandant of Justice Liang Xiang together with the Chancellor's Chief Clerk, the Palace Assistant Secretary, and five two-thousand-dan jointly tried the case of King Yun of Dongping; at the time the winter month had not yet completed two tens of days, yet Xiang in his heart suspected Yun was wronged; the prison case had embellished language; he memorialized wishing to transmit it to Chang'an and again send down to dukes and ministers to retry.
translations.idiomatic: Earlier, Commandant of Justice Liang Xiang, the chancellor's chief clerk, the palace assistant secretary, and five senior officials jointly tried King Yun of Dongping. Winter court had not yet run twenty days when Xiang began to doubt the king's guilt—the record looked cooked—and he asked to move the dossier to Chang'an for review by the high ministers.

sentences.zh: 尚書令鞫譚、僕射宗伯鳳以為可許。

translations.literal: Masters of Writing Director Ju Tan and Supervisor Zongbo Feng deemed it could be permitted.
translations.idiomatic: Director Ju Tan of the secretariat and Supervisor Zongbo Feng ruled that the request should be granted.

sentences.zh: 天子以相等皆見上體不平，外內顧望，操持兩心，幸雲踰冬，無討賊疾惡主讎之意，制詔免相等皆為庶人。

translations.literal: The Son of Heaven, because Xiang and the others all saw the sovereign's body was not at peace, looked outward and inward, held two hearts, hoped Yun would pass winter, had no mind to punish rebels, hate evil, and avenge the lord—by edict dismissed Xiang and the others all to be commoners.
translations.idiomatic: The emperor decided that Xiang and his colleagues, seeing the throne unsettled, had hedged their bets and dragged their feet so that Yun might live out the winter—showing no zeal to punish treason or avenge their sovereign—and stripped them all to commoner status.

sentences.zh: 後數月大赦，嘉奏封事薦相等明習治獄，「相計謀深沈，譚頗知雅文，鳳經明行修，聖王有計功除過，臣竊為朝廷惜此三人。」

translations.literal: After several months there was a great amnesty; Jia submitted a sealed memorial recommending Xiang and the others as clear and practiced in handling prisons: "Xiang's plans and strategies are deep and sunken; Tan is rather versed in elegant documents; Feng's classics are clear and conduct cultivated; sage kings have reckoned merit and removed faults—your servant secretly for the court pities these three men."
translations.idiomatic: Months later a general amnesty cleared their names; Jia sent a sealed memorial praising their skill in criminal justice—"Xiang is deep and steady, Tan masters court prose, Feng is a scholar of spotless conduct; sage kings weigh merit against fault—and your servant grieves that the court cast away such men."

sentences.zh: 書奏，上不能平。

translations.literal: When the memorial was submitted, the sovereign could not be at peace.
translations.idiomatic: The emperor read the memorial and seethed.

sentences.zh: 後二十餘日，嘉封還益董賢戶事，上乃發怒，召嘉詣尚書，責問以「

translations.literal: After more than twenty days, when Jia sealed and returned the matter of increasing Dong Xian's households, the sovereign then flared anger, summoned Jia to the Masters of Writing, and rebuked him with "
translations.idiomatic: Some twenty days later, when Jia again returned the edict enlarging Dong Xian's fief, the emperor exploded: he summoned Jia to the secretariat and began his rebuke with "

sentences.zh: 相等前坐在位不盡忠誠，外附諸侯，操持兩心，背人臣之義，今所稱相等材美，足以相計除罪。

translations.literal: Xiang and the others formerly sat in place without exhausting loyalty and sincerity; outwardly they attached to feudal lords; they held two hearts; they betrayed the righteousness of ministers; now what you praise of Xiang and the others' talent and beauty is sufficient thereby to reckon away guilt.
translations.idiomatic: Xiang and his colleagues once held office without loyalty, courted the feudal kings, and played both sides against the middle—violating every duty a minister owes. Are their talents now so splendid that they can erase those crimes by arithmetic?

sentences.zh: 君以道德，位在三公，以總方略一統萬類分明善惡為職，知相等罪惡陳列，著聞天下，時輒以自劾，今又稱譽相等，云為朝廷惜之。

translations.literal: You by virtue and the Way stand among the Three Dukes; your duty is to sum strategies and methods, unify the myriad kinds, and clarify good and evil; you knew Xiang and the others' crimes and evils arrayed, manifestly heard throughout the world; at the time you then impeached yourself; now again you praise Xiang and the others, saying you pity them for the court.
translations.idiomatic: You sit as chancellor on moral grounds: your charge is to set policy for the myriad affairs and distinguish right from wrong. You knew Xiang's guilt was notorious, impeached yourself at the time—yet now you praise those same men and say the court should regret losing them.

sentences.zh: 大臣舉錯，恣心自在，迷國罔上，近由君始，將謂遠者何！

translations.literal: Great ministers' lifting and setting aside follow heart and are self-indulgent; they delude the state and deceive the superior; near it begins with you—what shall we say of those far!
translations.idiomatic: When great ministers act on whim, delude the state, and mock the throne, the rot begins with you—what then of the ranks below?

sentences.zh: 對狀。」

translations.literal: Reply to the accusation document."
translations.idiomatic: Answer the charge in writing."

sentences.zh: 嘉免冠謝罪。

translations.literal: Jia removed cap and acknowledged guilt.
translations.idiomatic: Jia took off his cap and apologized.

sentences.zh: 事下將軍中朝者。

translations.literal: The matter was sent down to generals and those of the inner court.
translations.idiomatic: The case was referred to the generals and the inner court.
